metadata.title: Why they don’t trust the media: The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the social media era, taking the Liu Xuezhou incident as an example

abstract: With the advent of the post-pandemic era characterised by increasing social uncertainty, the public is placing higher demands on the trust in news media. According to the 2023 Edelman Trust Barometer, the image of the media in the public's mind is deteriorating. Why don't they trust the media? What are the reasons given by the public when the media is denounced as unscrupulous? Taking the Liu Xuezhou incident as a typical case, the study extracts key elements from 5,225 related microblog texts, identifies the main topics of public discussion with the help of LDA topic modelling, clarifies the referential dimension of unscrupulous media with the help of pointwise mutual information, and analyzes netizens' evaluations related to different referential levels of the media through visual semantic networks, which can help formulate dialectical thinking on media trust in the context of China in the era of social media.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
paragraphs: With the advent of the post-pandemic era, people's perceptions of risk have grown stronger, and so they expect to learn through the news what is really happening in society, how these problems and events are occurring, and what we should do to address them. Accordingly, the public will demand more credibility from the media. In 2023 Edelman published the latest Global Trust Barometer data, which show that governments and the media contribute to the cycle of distrust and are seen as sources of misinformation. Respondents in 16 out of 28 countries/territories around the world said they did not trust their government, and respondents in 15 countries said they did not trust their media. Compared to Western countries such as the UK and the US, Chinese citizens have a higher level of trust in government and media organisations, but this does not mean that China does not have a media trust problem. Due to the deep connection between the media and political power, journalism in China, as a professional field co-constructed by the state and the market, is still in the stage of a 'high degree of heteronomy' and 'low degree of autonomy', and the authority of journalism is gained by becoming part of the power structure. The public's trust in the news media is essentially a trust in the political power behind it rather than in the media's professional competence. In the age of social media, the technological form of the Internet itself has rewritten the composition of media trust, and Chinese journalism is unable to gain the public's trust solely on the basis of the endorsement of political power, which is a symptom of the problematisation of media trust in China.

This paper attempts to focus on the crisis of trust in the Chinese media in recent years through the typical case of the 'Liu Xuezhou Incident' and discusses why the media is no longer trusted by the public in the Chinese context. The Liu Xuezhou incident was chosen as a typical case to study the issue of media trust in China for the following reasons: in Chinese society, news media often plays the role of agenda setter carefully, so the media seldom gets involved in the whirlpool of public opinion. Even if they are attacked by netizens for inappropriate comments, the political forces behind the media will help them to 'minimise' the problem by deleting the posts. Unfortunately, in the Liu Xuezhou incident, the news media did not get away with their 'mistakes', and the topic of whether the media is trustworthy was pushed into the limelight by the protagonist's accusations against the media, making the Liu Xuezhou incident a typical case with the qualities of critical incidents and hot moments. Around this incident, Chinese netizens have had an extremely rich discussion about media trust, which is rare in Chinese society, and thus provides a valuable opportunity to explore the research question.

Event Review: In late 2021, Liu Xuezhou, a boy from Hebei, released a family search revelation on social media and successfully found his birth parents, during which he continued to update on his family search progress through microblogging and other platforms. At the beginning of 2022, Liu Xuezhou inadvertently learned that he had been sold by his birth parents to traffickers and that he had been blackmailed and cyber-violated by netizens because of his struggle to get a 'house' to live in from his parents. Many media outlets tracked this event in real time, and in the process, the wellknown media outlet The Beijing News was 'named' by Liu Xuezhou in a microblog screenshot for presenting only one side of Liu Xuezhou's birth parents' story. On 24 January 2022, 'the disliked Liu Xuezhou' ended his life, and The Beijing News was accused by netizens of being 'unscrupulous media' and a 'murderer' after originally being an agenda setter. According to the word cloud diagram of the big data platform Knowing and Seeing, the frequency of the terms 'news media' and 'The Beijing News' ranked at the top of all relevant words, which is not common in China's public opinion events. In view of the specificity and typicality of the incident, our study intends to select the original microblog comments of netizens related to this incident as the research object, to mine the potential themes in the text by establishing an LDA topic model, and to further analyse 'what the public is discussing' with the help of a semantic network to answer the question 'Why is the media not trusted?'

paragraphs: In the relevant literature, media trust is almost always discussed under the label of media credibility (Gaziano & McGrath, 1986;Metzger et al., 2003;Meyer, 1988). Scholars tend to materialise the holistic perception of media trust into various indicators of media credibility, and few of them continue to abstract the concept upwards, placing it under the overall social system and looking down on it, viewing the media as a subsystem and relating it to other subsystems. In order to explore the issue of why the media is not trusted, it is necessary to abstract the concept, introduce the sociological and philosophical perspectives into the specific context of journalism and communication, understand how the concepts of trust and media trust are constructed, and then go back to the specific context to discuss how they are deconstructed and demystified through empirical research, ultimately finding out the deeper crux of the problem behind the loss of media trust.

sections.header_raw: Trust: Western theory and Chinese issue
sections.header_norm: trust western theory and chinese issue
paragraphs: The study of trust in the field of sociology began in the 1970s, with Luhmann's 1979 concept being a classic work in the sociological study of trust. 'I trust' means that individuals attempt to reduce uncertainty by simplifying and setting aside potential complexity and uncertainty through trust relationships, thereby reducing the costs of interpersonal and social interactions. Based on Luhmann's research, Giddens continues to relate the issue of trust to the issue of risk and proposes the concept of 'disembeding'. He believes that modern society makes people detached from the traditional constant geographical boundaries, and since traditional knowledge habits cannot provide effective reference for people in modern society, people no longer rely on the specific living environment to obtain trust, and the trust mechanisms have transitioned from personal trust to system trust. System trust is divided into trust in symbolic tokens and trust in an expert system. Symbolic tokens refer to a medium of exchange that is removed from a specific context, such as currency, which is a universal medium of exchange that does not require mutual familiarity to be successful. The expert system is also no longer a familiar social relationship, where trust is based on their technical achievements, title, and professional knowledge, rather than on their personality and the good relations between them.

It is true that the discussion of trust began in the West, and its related theories can be used as a reference to compare with the characteristics of Chinese society and to find out what trust problems China is facing and what theoretical insights these problems can give us. However, trust as a universal social fact has distinctive cultural characteristics, and different cultures will have different assumptions about human nature, relationships, and people due to their own differences in natural environments and social compositions, which, in turn, generate different connotations and extensions of trust. Therefore, when interpreting the trust issue in China, we still need to start from a Chinese historical background and cultural context and take the typical factors that China possesses into consideration.

In China's vernacular society, the agrarian economy and the patriarchal system limit the farmers' range of activities, as land cannot be moved. Living in clusters emphasised harmony and stability, making interpersonal relationships more binding and deception more difficult, and thus trust issues were virtually non-existent in traditional Chinese agrarian societies. After the implementation of China's reform and opening-up policy, China has truly moved towards a modern society based on a market economy. The increasing mobility of the population has easily eroded the original foundation of relational trust, while new mechanisms for trusting strangers have not yet been established or have not been established well enough.

Against this backdrop, moral constraints can no longer maintain old trust relationships, and China urgently needs to establish a new trust system from an institutional perspective. As Francis L.K. Hsu explains in his comparative cultural studies, Americans are self-dependent. In their individualistic perspective, the more a person relies on his own self, the more he will value his own personality and ability and, simultaneously, the more he will trust the social system, as this lifestyle emphasises the relationship between the individual and society. Chinese people are interdependent, and under the influence of familial values, they stick together; meanwhile, they themselves could gain interests and benefits through seeking people with a strong sense of identity. From the above statement, we can reach a conclusion that compared with the contractual ethics of the West, China has emphasised the convenience and power of relationships since ancient times, and traditional Chinese morality has not been involved in the exploration of relationships with strangers. When the globalisation of the market forced China to move towards a risk society, individuals became 'tourists' or 'vagabonds' in Bauman's sense of a separated community. For strangers who meet and pass by in a hurry, 'we' have neither a collective memory nor a future to grasp together, and it becomes difficult for people to trust others. The bias towards interpersonal relationships in traditional Chinese morality is an obstacle to the establishment of institutional trust, which is a special feature of the trust issue in the Chinese context, while the media has repeatedly suffered from the hidden crux of the crisis of public trust in China.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
sections.header_raw: Media trust: Theoretical evidence and Chinese dilemma
sections.header_norm: media trust theoretical evidence and chinese dilemma
paragraphs: Media trust is an important branch of social trust, but in discussing the issue of news media and public trust, researchers have mostly approached the issue from two aspects: news authority, which is biased towards the producer's discourse construct (Carlson, 2017;Zelizer, 1992), and media trustworthiness, which is biased towards the measurement of news users and news consumption. Little research has been conducted on the relationship between journalistic practice itself and public trust in the news media, and even less attention has been paid to how it arises, as if media trust were a self-evident fact. If we trace the process and history of the emergence of media trust, it has two types of generative mechanismsattribute-based trust and process-based trustand will eventually stabilise to form a kind of institutional trust in the media. First, public trust in news media often stems from trust in its professional functions, which means that the media can fully perform functions such as information provision, investigative reporting, analytical commentary, social sympathy, public forums, social mobilisation, and propagation of representative democracy. In this process, journalists and news organisations also gain the power to accurately, truthfully, and objectively interpret social reality by the innate proximity of the media, constructing the so-called news authority. Second, repeated exposure to the media by audiences will further strengthen and consolidate media trust with feedback through newspaper subscriptions, television ratings, and the number of views and clicks on individual stories, forming a process-based trust mechanism. Finally, audiences will gradually develop institutional trust and dependence on the entire media system. This relationship is directly proportional to the complexity of the social structure and inversely proportional to the degree of social pluralism.

Institution-based media trust implies that media trust is closely related to the social and institutional ecology in which the media are embedded. Whereas the professionalisation of the journalism in the West gained exclusive power over the collection and dissemination of facts through the veneration of journalistic objectivity, journalism in China follows a mainly political logic, and its journalistic power is gained by becoming part of the political system. This situation improved after 1978, due to the gradual relaxation of state control, as Chinese journalism gradually shifted from positive freedom (actively promoting the ideology of the CCP) to negative freedom (not antagonising the CCP's ideology) prior to the reform and opening up of China. The pull of the market and the awakening professionalism of media journalists prompted the media to strive to expand their own reporting space and turn issues of public concern into public issues, pushing for changes in the entire national policy and legal system. A series of news media with investigative reporting as their main product, such as the China Youth Daily and the Southern Weekly, flourished in the 1990s and assumed the role of a platform for social opinion in addition to their basic function as a mouthpiece, thus gradually moving towards professionalisation of the Chinese journalism industry.

However, with the advent of the Internet, the context of communication has been completely changed, and a wave of de-authorisation, de-centring, diversification, and fragmentation has arisen amidst the clamour of many voices. On the one hand, the 'visibility' of communication has increased, and the audience's criticisms of journalists, reports, or media no longer lie in the suggestion box but break through the barriers of the paper media era and are openly and intensively displayed in front of the public, so the controversial subjects of the event reports can change the preset agendas of the news media through their own voices. On the other hand, the empowerment of individuals by network technology has brought content production into the era of universal, fully organised, and socialised media, where professional organisations and non-professional individuals seek balance between cooperation and conflict to achieve communal production, and production logics where traffic is king have led to a proliferation of media misconducts, such as post-truth and reversal news. In the context of the Internet, the media cannot logically gain public recognition by virtue of their previous political shade, and while the forces of de-professionalisation are raging, the professionalisation of China's journalism industry has not yet been completed. As a public perception, 'the media is not trustworthy' reflects people's questioning of systemic trust, which highlights the maladaptation between people's behavioural obedience to expert systems and the basis of their psychological trust, and shows the rupture between technocracy and public communication in modern society.

Little research has been conducted on the relationship between journalistic practice itself and public trust in the news media, and even less attention has been paid to how it arises, as if media trust were a self-evident fact. If we trace the process and history of the emergence of media trust, it has two types of generative mechanismsattribute-based trust and process-based trustand will eventually stabilise to form a kind of institutional trust in the media. First, public trust in news media often stems from trust in its professional functions, which means that the media can fully perform functions such as information provision, investigative reporting, analytical commentary, social sympathy, public forums, social mobilisation, and propagation of representative democracy. In this process, journalists and news organisations also gain the power to accurately, truthfully, and objectively interpret social reality by the innate proximity of the media, constructing the so-called news authority. Second, repeated exposure to the media by audiences will further strengthen and consolidate media trust with feedback through newspaper subscriptions, television ratings, and the number of views and clicks on individual stories, forming a process-based trust mechanism. Finally, audiences will gradually develop institutional trust and dependence on the entire media system. paragraphs: As noted above, research on media trust has centred almost exclusively around the measurement of media credibility. This tradition began with the study of source credibility by Hovland et al. According to Hovland et al., the credibility of a communicator is based on two determinants: the audience's perception of the communicator's trustworthiness and the audience's perception of the communicator's expertness. However, Hovland et al.'s study only focuses on the trustworthiness of the source in persuasive communication and does not pay attention to the trustworthiness of the media. Research that really focuses on media credibility can be traced back to Burns W. Roper's seminal study on the credibility of radio, television, newspapers, and magazines. Beginning in 1959, every two years US audiences were asked a question: 'If you got conflicting or different reports of the same story from radio, television, the magazines and the newspapers, which of the four versions would you be most inclined to believe-the one on radio or television or magazines or newspapers?' Until 1959, newspapers remained the most credible source of information, but Roper's data later revealed a clear 'credibility advantage' for television. Despite the landmark status of this study in history, sharp criticism has been levelled against the Roper question, arguing that the biggest problem lies in treating media source credibility as a unidimensional concept.

After the 1970s, many researchers attempted to extract the measurement dimensions of media credibility by using factor analysis. In 1985, the American Society of Newspaper Editors (ASNE) conducted a nationwide telephone survey. It designed a five-level scale with 16 indicator items and analysed the results to summarise the 12 constituent items of media credibility. Meyer then reanalysed the raw data from the ANSE, tested their reliability, and finally came up with five pairs of adjectives for 'credibility' and four for 'social affiliation', which became a scale with high reliability. In the 21st century, the rapid development of the Internet has further promoted the new process of media credibility research, and scholars are still striving to validate and develop new multidimensional scales. For example, Kohring and Matthes (2007) argue that previous studies have neglected the element of 'selectivity', and thus they define four separate dimensions of public trust in a particular media from the perspective of public selectivity. Yale et al. (2015) also try to study the firstand second-order factor structures of media credibility and conclude that it is not possible to empirically establish that media credibility consists of different subdimensions. Instead, they recommend that any credibility scale 'must be treated as a single-factor measure when used as a variable'.

Just as academics are debating over the scale issue, some scholars have taken an alternative approach, pointing out that the design of closed-ended question items under the topic of trust in media is not sufficient to reveal people's actual thoughts, and that the public should be allowed to freely formulate open-ended answers on the issue (Fisher, 2016;Daniller et al., 2017;Knudsen et al., 2022). For example, Kundsen et al. collected 1,500 written responses through a Norwegian online probability-based survey and used a semi-supervised quantitative text analysis technique called structural topic modelling to uncover potential themes in citizens' responses, drawing on the dual strengths of both qualitative and quantitative research: a richly detailed sample and both generalisability and objectivity. Compared to the pluralistic nature of relevant studies in the West, Chinese research on media trust is still in the developmental stage, and the research situation can be summarised by four features: more discussion of normative theories and less positivist research; more reviews of relevant literature for the West and less sorting out of local empirical studies; confused use of concepts and lack of operationalisation and consistency of scales; and the exploration of the issue of trust still focuses on the political level and lacks attention to the individual members of the populace.

paragraphs: Combining the a priori observations of the Liu Xuezhou incident with the theoretical shortcomings of the literature, this research raises the following research questions (RQs):

RQ1. What are the key points of the public's discussion when they refer to 'Liu Xuezhou' and 'the media' in their related texts? RQ2. What dimension of the media concept will be referred to as 'unscrupulous media' from the public, and what reasons will be given from the public when criticising the media as 'unscrupulous media?' RQ3. How can the media rebuild trust with the public when faced with this situation?

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
paragraphs: This paper establishes an experimental framework using a three-step, problem-oriented approach based on a comprehensive review of relevant literature. First, recognising topic modelling as a highly effective technique in text mining for data extraction, latent information discovery, and relationship identification among data and text documents, we employed this method to address RQ1. Next, we focused our text analysis on documents that mention 'unscrupulous media', applying Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) to elucidate the public's perspective on this concept. The PMI method highlights the interconnections between variables within complex systems. Its application in analysing social media interactions can uncover significant relationships that may not be evident through superficial examination. Following the establishment of the referential dimension of 'unscrupulous media', we will map semantic networks to analyse netizens' evaluations of various media dimensions, ultimately providing a comprehensive answer to RQ2. By synthesising the results of these analyses, this study aims to offer a dialectical reflection on media trust in the context of social media in China, thereby addressing RQ3.

The research framework shown in Figure 1 summarises the overall process and argumentative logic of the empirical study. The parts of data collection and pre-processing, LDA topic modelling, and PMI are all implemented in a Python environment, while the semantic network analysis and the visualisation of each link are done by Gephi. Naming the topics, clarifying the conceptual pointing of the key information, and establishing the attribution system have to be carried out manually.

paragraphs: In this paper, we adopt an automated content analysis methodology based on three algorithms: LDA topic modelling, PMI, and semantic network analysis. In addition, we use Gephi, a JVM-based web analysis software, to visualise the data.

LDA topic modelling. As an unsupervised machine learning technique, LDA is able to automatically mine topic information from massive text data. It uses a bag of words to transform each document into word frequency vectors, which turn textual information into digital information that is easy to model. In the big data environment, the use of an LDA model can help researchers to perform potential topic identification and user clustering analysis. In this paper, perplexity is used as an evaluation metric to determine the optimal number of topics in a document. Perplexity is widely used to evaluate the prediction performance of probabilistic models on the acquired samples and can also be used to optimise the selection of the number of topics. The formula is as follows

where D denotes the set of all words in a document; M denotes the number of documents; Wd denotes the words in document d; Nd denotes the number of words in each document d; and PWd denotes the probability of occurrence of a word in a document. Perplexity values generally show a decreasing law with the increase of the number of potential topics, and the smaller the value of perplexity, the stronger the generative ability of the topic model. Therefore, this paper selects the topic values with smaller perplexity and fewer topics as the optimal parameters for LDA model training.

Pointwise mutual information. Pointwise mutual information (PMI) is a measure of association that looks at particular instances of the two random variables word1 and word2. The formula is given in the following equation PMIðword1, word2Þ ¼ log 2
Pðword1&word2Þ Pðword1ÞPðword2Þ

In the formula, Pword1&word2 denotes the probability that two words word1 and word2 occur together, and Pword1 and Pword2 denote the probability that the two words occur separately. When PMI>0, the two words are correlated; when PMI = 0, the two words are not correlated or mutually exclusive; when PMI<0, the two words are totally not correlated. It can measure the difference between the probability of their co-occurrence given their joint distribution and the probability of their co-occurrence given the marginal distributions of word1 and word2, thus assuming the two random variables are independent.

Semantic network analysis. Semantic network analysis methods can rely on computer technology to achieve word extraction, word relationship analysis, and visualisation of text (or corpus). In the semantic network diagram, the connection between any two words represents a proposition. And the proposition reflects the connection of things in the mind of the text producer and the facts he tries to present to the reader. In essence, a semantic network is a logical map governed by the perceptions of the text producer, which can be used to restore the intentions of the text producer. Specific to the netizen comments of an opinion event, semantic network analysis can help the researcher to generalise certain consensus from the diverse user-produced texts.

Gephi is a data visualisation software focussing on the field of network analysis, which applies network layout algorithms to transform network relationships into graphs and calculates the network attributes through a variety of statistical algorithms, which provides a powerful support to study the characteristics of semantic networks. It supports intuition-oriented analysis by network manipulations in real time and is capable of revealing the underlying structures of associations between research objects. This software will be used in this study to visualise the results of LDA topic modelling as well as pointwise mutual information calculations.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
paragraphs: In terms of data acquisition, the study selected Sina Weibo as the data source. The reason for the selection is that the public opinion controversy of this incident started from Weibo, and key nodes such as Liu Xuezhou and the Beijing News can be traced on this platform, so Weibo is a representative object of investigation, both in terms of the influence of content and the volume of public opinion. After confirming the platform, this study utilises the Scrapy library in Python as a tool and selects original microblogs containing the keywords 'Liu Xuezhou' and 'media' as the source of information, obtaining the fields including the date and content of users' postings. It should be emphasised that in answering the question of why the media is not trusted in the context of the Liu Xuezhou incident, it is important to qualify the notion of 'the public' to make it knowable. In China, not all members of the public have their own position on the issue of media trust, and those who actively talk about the issue on social media platforms tend to be the most concerned group. Therefore, while the study fails to break down the concept of the Chinese public to capture the views of different demographic groups on this issue, it focuses on the event itself and takes into account the netizen's mobility by filtering the netizen comment samples based on the criterion of 'active expression of relevant opinions', which allows the researcher to study a specific sample of netizens in depth in a given context. Furthermore, compared with the short comments of netizens, netizens who posted original microblogs are more concerned about the media performance in the Liu Xuezhou incident and are more eager to express their opinions on this topic, so the clustering of the samples can make the results more typical. In terms of the selection of the collection time period, the day of Liu Xuezhou's death, 24 January 2022, was taken as the starting point, and one year after the incident, 24 January 2023, was taken as the ending point. A total of 5,225 microblogs were obtained in the end, with a total of more than 910,000 words.

Subsequently, the study removes the stop words that have a high frequency of repetition and are likely to affect the distribution of subjects with the help of commonly used Chinese stopwords, such as hit_stopwords, scu_stopwords, and baidu_stopwords. In addition, considering that hashtags in the content of blog posts can interfere with the final results of the data, this study removes the hashtags by writing regular expressions. Finally, we use 'jieba', which is a Python Chinese word segmentation module to segment the text data and extract nouns, nominal morphemes, verbs, nominal verbs, proper names, and idioms by repeated experiments. Examples of subwords are shown in Table 1. Pre-processing the data helped this study to obtain a higher-quality text, which can provide effective support for subsequent modelling.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
paragraphs: The results of the study will be presented through three steps: modelling LDA topics on the original microblog text, establishing words that are associated with 'unscrupulous media' based on PMI, and visualising semantic networks to analyse the different referential levels of the abstract concept of media.

Thematic clustering results for the original text of microblogs. In this paper, we propose to take the integers in the interval from 1 to 15 as the candidate number of topics and calculate the value of perplexity corresponding to a different number of topics. As shown in Figure 2, the perplexity degree demonstrates an overall trend of rapidly decreasing and then fluctuating upward, and the lowest point of the value that corresponds to the optimal number of topics is 3. Therefore, this paper proposes to select 3 as the topic parameter value of the LDA topic model.

At the same time, this paper randomly selects six documents to draw the document-topic distribution graph, as shown in Figure 3. From the figure, it can be seen that the probability of a certain topic in the extracted documents is higher than other topics, which means that there is a major topic in the document, further verifying the effectiveness of the model for the topic division of microblog text.

As shown in Table 2, combining the theme words and the content of the microblog text to summarise the topic, this paper describes Topic #0 as 'Discussion around news parties', with a weight of 19.39%; Topic #1 as 'Evaluation around news media organisations and journalists', with a weight of 63.27%; and Topic #2 as 'Criticism around cyber violence', with a weight of 17.35%.

Topic #0 'Discussions around news parties'. The core words of the topic can be attributed to various elements of the news event, which clearly presents Liu Xuezhou's life trajectory: he was born and sold by his biological parents to human traffickers in exchange for a betrothal gift, bullied by his classmates at school, molested by his male teacher, raped by netizens in microblog messages because he asked his parents for a house, and killed himself after writing a suicide note on his personal account. In the related text, we can find netizens expressing their sympathy by relaying his experience, while condemning the responsible parties in this news event. Topic #1 'Evaluation of news media organisations and journalists'. Under this topic, netizens criticised individual media organisations and the whole press. From the core words, we summarise some of the reasons why people are dissatisfied with the media, like 'only caring about the traffic', 'maliciously guiding public opinion', and 'leading netstorm'. In addition, words such as 'facts', 'responsibility', 'justice', and 'investigation' remain professional guidelines that the media should always keep in mind from a public perspective.

Topic #2 'Criticism around cyber violence'. This theme centres on the ecology of online public opinion, in which netizens, out of moral indignation, regard 'unscrupulous media' and 'keyboard warriors' as 'murderers' who 'invert right and wrong' and eat 'human blood buns'. One netizen's comment summarises this theme: 'Again and again, only when people die, the media will be "truthful," netizens will be "kind," and only when the tide recedes and you see the scaly body under the water will you truly feel sad for him'. As can be seen in Figure 4, the co-occurrence network drawn in Gephi consists of four themes -'news', 'media', 'unscrupulous media', and 'The Beijing News'which are in the centre of the map, linking Liu Xuezhou's individual experience and netizens' discussion of the whole public opinion ecology. Amidst the maelstrom of public opinion, we need to think about the role of news media as a meso level between the individual and the society. Words associated with 'unscrupulous media' based on PMI. From the above topic modelling results, it is clear that the public has a critical attitude towards 'media'. At the same time, the study also found that the 'media' in the netizens' view not only refers to individual media organisations but also involves other dimensions of the media concept, so we will further explore two questions: Which dimension of the media concept is the 'unscrupulous media' referred to by the public? What reasons are given by the public when the media are denounced as 'unscrupulous media?' In order to solve these problems, this paper uses PMI to further screen documents containing the keyword 'unscrupulous media' in the document dataset and calculate the semantic similarity between the words. When the PMI index between words is higher than 0, there is a correlation between them. Figure 5 shows all 49 words related to the term 'unscrupulous media', among which the words 'news', 'report', 'reporter', 'media', 'The Beijing News', and 'the self-operating network account' are listed. It can be seen that when the public refers to 'unscrupulous media', they are referring to the three levels of the media concept: the content of reports, journalists, and media organisations. In addition to professional media, they also cover online self-media, such as 'the self-operating network account'.

Semantic network analysis of referential dimensions of media. From the previous section, it is clear that the media is a concept with a high level of abstraction that encompasses the whole of journalism, different media organisations, journalists, and news reporting at multiple levels. When people mistrust the media and accuse them of being 'unscrupulous', different individuals may refer to different levels of media references. When confronted with these comments, either concluding that the Chinese public has lost confidence in Chinese journalism, or simply blaming individual media organisations for this phenomenon, is a one-sided understanding of the issue of media trust. The question of why the media is not trusted can only be answered by clarifying the forms in which the different concepts of media appear in netizens' texts. Therefore, in order to further explore how netizens' texts talk about and evaluate the different references of 'media', the study will analyse the texts in depth by mapping the semantic network analysis.

The study selected the top fifty words with the highest frequency in the text, constructed a word co-occurrence matrix by calculating the PMI index between any two words, and used Force Atlas, a layout algorithm in Gephi, to realise the visualisation operation and convert the co-occurrence matrix into a semantic network composed of word nodes and edges. In this network, the centrality of a node can be used to measure the importance of a keyword, the higher the degree of centrality, the more the node co-occurs with other nodes, and the larger the node and label fonts, which will form a visual 'semantic cluster'. Similarly, the thickness of the edges can be set according to the degree centrality of the node, and the thicker the edges, the more frequent the co-occurrence between two nodes. In addition, the researcher believes that the proposition formed by two words reflects the connection of things in the mind of the text producer, and this connection is usually reflected in a directed linear logic, so the study especially labelled the directionality of the edges between the nodes, which enables the researcher to more clearly identify the directionality of the relationship between words.

First, the study screened all the words related to the media referent level from the source nodes, which are media, news, the Beijing News, reporter, the self-operating network account, and we media. Subsequently, the researcher used these words as source nodes to filter out all target nodes related to them and carried out thematic clustering based on semantic relevance, resulting in three semantic clusters as shown in the figure. As can be seen from Figure 6, the source node centred on 'reporter' co-occurs more often with its multiple target nodes, and when netizens mention reporterrelated topics, they focus on three aspects: firstly, the relationship between journalists and the parties involved in the incident, such as 'parents' and 'schools'; secondly, the norms that journalists are supposed to abide by (e.g. 'objectivity' and 'justice') and their actual behaviours (e.g. 'leading the beat' and chasing 'heat'); and finally, the requirement to 'remediate' journalists' misbehaviour. Unlike the semantic cluster of 'journalist', in the cluster centred on 'the self-operating network account', there are two types of words that co-occur more frequently with the central word: 'we media', 'account', and other information content publishers, as well as netizens' extremely negative comments on we media represented by self-operating network accounts, such as 'disgusting', 'unscrupulous media', and 'keyboard warrior'. For the misbehaviour of self-operating network accounts and other we media, netizens only blame them, rather than ordering them to make corrections or giving them constructive suggestions. Finally, in the semantic cluster centred on 'news', 'media', and 'the Beijing News', the most edges start from 'media' as the source node, which indicates that 'media' exists as a dominant topic.. In addition, in this cluster, there are many overlaps between the target nodes of 'the Beijing News' and 'News' and the target node of 'Journalist' as the source node, but unlike the latter, 'News' and 'the Beijing News' also point to the nodes of 'Society', 'Law', 'Public Opinion', and 'Responsibility', which implies that when people discuss 'the Beijing News' and 'News', they pay more attention to their responsibility in shaping the environment of public opinion. between professional and non-professional media has become more blurred, and when talking about media, not only professional media but also we media such as the self-operating network numbers are associated. It is noteworthy that in the evaluation system of netizens, the we media are expected to have low expectations and do not need to bear much social responsibility, and the negative consequences caused by their misbehaviour are often borne by the professional media.

sections.paper_id: Ye and Sun - 2024 - Why they don’t trust the media The Chinese public’s perception and evaluation of news media in the
paragraphs: Reasons: Why the media is not trusted by the public Journalist-media-organisation-journalism: Public perceptions and imaginaries of the 'media'. In Liu Xuezhou incident, the media were blamed for the journalists' failure to report objectively. As a result, journalists were caught in the centre of the 'unscrupulous media' vortex of public opinion. 'A journalist's pen is worth 3,000 Mauser rifles'. In reviewing the empirical materials, the researcher found that many netizens used Napoleon's famous quote to describe their own understanding of the profession of journalists. In the public's perception, journalists are the relayers of news facts, the agenda setters, and the opinion leaders. The public's acquiescence to journalists' privileged discourse also means that they place their trust in the media, and when journalist's actions deviate from the public's imagination of the role as a journalist, the public is inclined to interpret this deviation as a kind of betrayal and then announce on social media that they have withdrawn their trust in the media. As a result, not only do journalists face a crisis of professional reputation, but the media organisations face a loss of brand reputation due to lax gatekeeping, and journalism as a whole is criticised by the public for its lack of accountability. Finally, the term 'unscrupulous media' is frequently mentioned in the cycle of public criticism.

Behind media discredit: The misplaced weight of content and operations in platformised news production. In this research, the words 'trending' and 'traffic' have become high-frequency words used by the public to describe media misbehaviour. So, how should we look at the relationship between media and traffic? Nowadays, digital technology has amplified the weight of information distribution in the generation of influence, and the platform itself can obtain huge traffic by virtue of self-producing and self-selling user's information. The media is merely embedded in the platform as one of the nodes, reduced to a 'content provider', passively adapting to the logic and rules of the platform. Against this backdrop, the media-operation departments usually play a pivotal role as intermediaries linking editorial teams with third-party platforms: they constantly 'look out' for the latest hot events and topics on the platforms, and use traffic as a guide to determine the news content that editorial teams need to produce. At this point, the standard of journalistic professionalism has been reduced to a tactic of boasting one's own professionalism, the purpose of which is not to ensure that journalists' news reports conform to the facts as much as possible, but rather to define the boundaries between the professional media and non-professional media, and to continuously consolidate one's own professional authority. When professionalism, which was originally at the centre of news production, is reduced to a strategy, the crisis behind it is self-evident.

Consequences of lost boundaries: Self-operating network accounts as a media will exacerbate the crisis of media trust. Through the comparison of the two semantic network diagrams, we can see that the public's evaluation of the media includes the criticisms of the we media and self-operating network accounts. Along with the deep development of social media, the public gradually includes selfoperating network accounts in the category of media, and the boundary between professional media and non-professional media is gradually blurred. Unlike professional media, the motive of the selfoperating network account to chase traffic is very obvious, and they have no intention to boast their professional authority. Accordingly, the public maintains a wary attitude and low expectations towards them. Even when they distort facts, the public usually responds with scolding and does not demand too much from them. However, the bad influence caused by self-operating network accounts does not disappear into thin air but is subsumed into the public's perception of the media, little by little eroding the public's trust in the media. Therefore, to solve this problem, it is necessary to improve the regulation and requirements for self-operating network accounts to prevent deterioration of public perception of the media.

Behind the 'knowingly wrong': The deep binding of media and political power. After the Liu Xuezhou incident, many netizens suggested that the Beijing News owed the victim and the public an apology, and that the relevant departments needed to punish it severely. However, what netizens waited for was not a clear clarification or apology from the newspaper but rather the opening of a 'comment selection' under the relevant microblog. The cold treatment of Liu Xuezhou incident by the Beijing News easily reminds people of the lack of 'corrective defense principle' in the current journalism industry. Few media take the initiative to speak out after making a mistake, and most of the media are afraid of triggering secondary public opinion storms that would jeopardise their own 'credibility'. This brings us back to the question of where the 'credibility' of China's mainstream media comes from: the media and state power go hand in hand, with the media gaining power through their official backgrounds, and the political power relying on the media to build public opinion positions. When the mainstream media chooses to apologise, it means facing an unknown response from the public, and if the media loses its authority, it will further jeopardise the political authority behind it. For various security reasons, media usually chooses to deal with it coldly or not to respond.

It is true that China's media have been marketised for more than three decades, and that the state has gradually relaxed its control, allowing the journalism to move from the positive freedom of promoting the ideology of the CCP to the negative freedom of not opposing the CCP's ideology. However, because the scope of 'not opposing the ideology of the CPC' is too general, there are more and more 'minefields' that cannot be touched, and the tacit awareness and concept of 'playing safe' have gradually been internalised in the practical logic of Chinese journalism, which has become the deep-seated reason behind the cold treatment.

It is true that China's media have been marketised for more than three decades, and that the state has gradually relaxed its control, allowing the journalism to move from the positive freedom of promoting the ideology of the CCP to the negative freedom of not opposing the CCP's ideology. However, because the scope of 'not opposing the ideology of the CPC' is too general, there are more and more 'minefields' that cannot be touched, and the tacit awareness and concept of 'playing safe' have gradually been internalised in the practical logic of Chinese journalism, which has become the deep-seated reason behind the cold treatment.

sections.header_raw: An alternative solution: A dialectical view of loss of trust in the media
sections.header_norm: an alternative solution a dialectical view of loss of trust in the media
paragraphs: Currently, China's mainstream media are in a network era that is unprecedentedly complex and far from being fully structured. Instead of occupying a central position and controlling the sources and channels of information dissemination, the media is subject to the constraints of political and capital logic. In the relationship between the media and the public, the media's innate advantage of trust is gradually weakening, and the public no longer looks up to the media but rather examines it in terms of general social units and general logic. However, the decline in media trust is not necessarily a regression; it marks a shift from system-based trust to attribute-and process-based trust, with positive trust factors that are actively created and heartfelt. This reflects the fact that the public no longer assigns trust to the media solely on the basis of the media's brand, history, or attributes of the state institution but rather makes cumulative trust judgements based on the assessment of each media behaviour. Therefore, the Internet, as a means of dissolving traditional trust, may be able to shape positive trust by virtue of reconfiguring legitimacy and promoting democratic dialogue, revitalising multiple forms of social interaction, and thus compensating for the lack of interpersonal and systemic trust. Whether there is a middle ground between confrontation and dialogue, division and integration, and dissolution and reconstruction depends on how we view the role played by the Internet in social trust relations.
